sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.intro: This certification scope covers the **SRE-Agent** (ID: sre-agent-v2.1), an AI-driven site reliability engineering assistant designed to support automated incident handling in production-like environme

content.body: This certification scope covers the **SRE-Agent** (ID: sre-agent-v2.1), an AI-driven site reliability engineering assistant designed to support automated incident handling in production-like environments. The evaluation encompassed **3** fault categories—Application, Network, and Resource—specifically including **node-restart**, **pod-delete**, **pod-dns-error**, **pod-network-corruption**, **pod-network-loss**, **pod-network-rate-limit**, **disk-fill**, **pod-autoscaler**, **pod-cpu-hog**, and **pod-memory-hog**. A total of **300** runs were executed, with **10** distinct faults exercised at **30** runs per fault. The assessment focused on the agent’s capabilities in fault detection, root-cause diagnosis, remediation planning and execution, and adherence to operational and safety compliance constraints, using a Multi-judge LLM Council with k=3 judges plus meta-reconciliation as the evaluation methodology.

Statistical adequacy. Each fault category contains ≥ 30 independent runs, meeting the framework-mandated minimum for the H-01 – H-09 statistical hypothesis tests. All nine hypotheses were evaluated at full strength.

sections.title: Evaluation Methodology
sections.intro: Qualitative assessments are produced by a council of 3 independent LLM judges. Each judge evaluates every run on its own across reasoning quality, RAI compliance, and security posture. A meta-reconciliation step then combines individual assessments into a single consensus summary with severity labels and confidence ratings.

items.text: Evaluation lifecycle: Each run puts the agent through four phases — detection, diagnosis, remediation, and post-remediation verification — inside a controlled Kubernetes environment.
Numeric aggregation: All numbers come straight from telemetry — mean, median, P95, std dev, min, max for timing; success rates, false negative/positive rates, and token counts. No manual adjustments.

Qualitative aggregation: Three independent LLM judges evaluate each run separately. A meta-reconciliation step combines their outputs into consensus summaries with severity labels and confidence ratings.
Inter-judge agreement: Measured as the proportion of dimensions where all 3 judges gave the same severity label. An agreement of 1.0 means every judge agreed on every dimension for every run.

Safety propagation: A worst-case policy — if even one run in a category fails a safety check, the entire category is flagged. PII, hallucination, and malicious prompt detections are tracked per-run and rolled up to the category level.

Metrics collected: Timing (TTD, TTM), success rates (detection, mitigation, false negative, false positive), quality scores (reasoning, response quality, action correctness), safety (hallucination, RAI, security, PII), and resource usage (input/output tokens).
Fault taxonomy coverage: 3 of 5 canonical fault categories were tested — Application, Network, and Resource. Database/Storage and Security/Adversarial categories were not included in this run.

Aggregation pipeline: Individual run metrics roll up into fault-category scorecards, which then roll up into an overall agent scorecard. Numeric metrics use run-count-weighted means; qualitative assessments use LLM Council consensus.
Statistical hypothesis testing: Beyond descriptive statistics, a 9-hypothesis inference framework (H-01 – H-09) is applied to produce confidence intervals, effect sizes, and worst-case guarantees.
Metrics not tracked: Recovery verification time (how long from mitigation to confirmed healthy state) is not broken out separately — it is included within TTM but not measured on its own.

Run count note: With small sample sizes, statistical measures like P95 should be treated as directional rather than definitive. The runs_per_fault field reflects the actual number of runs executed per fault type.

content.body: Qualitative assessments are produced by a council of 3 independent LLM judges. Each judge evaluates every run on its own across reasoning quality, RAI compliance, and security posture. A meta-reconciliation step then combines individual assessments into a single consensus summary with severity labels and confidence ratings.

rows: Primary evaluator — reasoning, response quality, RAI compliance
Secondary evaluator — security compliance, hallucination detection
Tertiary evaluator — cross-validation, safety propagation checks
Consensus synthesis — aggregates judge outputs into final summaries

content.body: Beyond descriptive statistics, this certification applies a formal 9-hypothesis inference framework grounded in 20 peer-reviewed papers (NeurIPS, ICLR, AAAI, ACL, ICSE, CCS, JRSS-B, JASA, Biometrika, Mathematical Finance). Each hypothesis replaces single-number summaries with probabilistic inference — confidence intervals, effect sizes, and worst-case guarantees — enabling defensible pass / conditional / fail decisions. Hypotheses H-01 to H-05 are always active; H-06 & H-07 activate only when SLA thresholds are provided; H-08 & H-09 provide informational tail-risk and temporal-stability analysis in both modes.

rows: How fast does the agent respond — and how confident are we?
What % of faults are caught — and what's the worst-case guarantee?
Does the agent handle all fault types equally well?
Does the agent fail some fault types more often?
Is the agent reliable every time, or erratic?
Can we prove this agent meets the SLA?
Wilcoxon signed-rank, TOST, Bootstrap BCa CI
Is the SLA violation rate below the allowed limit?
When the agent fails, how badly does it fail?
Is the agent getting worse over time?
content.title: H-01 – H-09 Framework Reference
content.footnote: * H-06 and H-07 require SLA thresholds to be configured. H-08 and H-09 provide informational analysis even without SLAs.

sections.title: Scorecard Snapshot
sections.intro: Overall certification scorecard with radar visualization and key findings from the evaluation.

items.text: Perfect threat detection coverage: The system achieved an overall detection rate of 100.0% (300 of 300 runs) across Application, Network, and Resource categories, with 0% false negatives in every category and a high Normalized TTD score of 0.95.

Mitigation effectiveness uneven: While overall mitigation rate is reported as 100% (300 of 300 runs), per-category mitigation percentages are notably lower—67% for Application, 49% for Network, and 61% for Resource—indicating inconsistency between aggregate and category-level mitigation performance.

Strong reasoning quality: Reasoning performance is robust, with an overall average reasoning score of 7.89/10 and per-category scores of 7.94 (Application), 7.75 (Network), and 7.99 (Resource), supported by a Normalized Reasoning score of 0.79.

Moderate response latency: Median time to detect (TTD) is relatively low at 44s for Application, 140s for Network, and 109s for Resource, but median time to mitigate (TTM) is higher at 161s, 305s, and 225s respectively, reflected in a Normalized TTM of 0.87.

Low hallucination behavior: Hallucination levels are low overall, with mean scores of 0.180 (Application), 0.175 (Network), and 0.187 (Resource), maximum hallucination values capped at 0.50, and a strong Normalized Hallucination score of 0.98.

RAI adherence moderately high: Responsible AI (RAI) compliance is generally solid but not perfect, with RAI rates of 77% for Application, 81% for Network, and 80% for Resource, and an overall Normalized Safety (RAI) score of 0.79.

Security performance slightly variable: Security metrics are strong but uneven across categories, with scores of 87% for Application, 78% for Network, and 83% for Resource, aligning with an overall Normalized Security score of 0.83.

Detection and mitigation latencies are materially higher for network faults — BCa IQM time-to-detect is 139.50s for network vs 42.77s (application) and 109.58s (resource), and IQM time-to-mitigate is 285.16s vs 195.14s and 242.71s respectively, with Kruskal-Wallis p < 0.001 confirming category-level differences.

Network mitigation reliability is the weakest among categories — fault mitigation success is 49.2% for network faults versus 66.7% (application) and 60.8% (resource), with H-04 chi-square p = 0.050 indicating statistically significant variation and identifying network as the weakest segment.

Detection success is generally strong but uneven across fault types — application and resource detection rates are 86.7% and 82.5% respectively, while network detection lags at 73.3%, with Wilson floors as low as 64.8% for network faults, indicating a lower bound on expected detection performance in that category.

Tool usage and core reasoning metrics are stable across categories — tool call counts show homogeneous variance (Levene p = 0.653, CV ≈ 0.22) and Kruskal-Wallis p ≥ 0.190 for reasoning quality and hallucination scores, indicating no statistically detectable cross-category differences in these cognitive dimensions.

Tail-risk remains material for detection latency and hallucinations despite acceptable central tendencies — H-08 flags significant tail risk for time-to-detect and hallucination scores, and H-09 detects drift in reasoning quality, implying rare but impactful failures that are not captured by median or IQM-based summaries.

sections.title: Part I — Agent Capability Assessment
sections.intro: Foundational quantitative + qualitative assessment of the agent's behaviour.

sections.title: Overall Qualitative Findings (LLM Council Output)
sections.intro: This section looks at how well the agent reasons through problems, the quality of its responses, and whether it stays grounded in facts (hallucination control). Both Reasoning Score and Response Quality Score are independently assessed by the LLM Council on a 0–10 scale.

items.text: Strong reasoning across domains: LLM Council rated reasoning as Strong with High confidence and agreement 1.0 for Application, Network, and Resource categories.
High reasoning quality scores: Reasoning and response quality scores were 7.94 (Application), 7.75 (Network), and 7.99 (Resource), contributing to a normalized Reasoning score of 0.79.
Strong RAI compliance overall: LLM Council rated RAI compliance as Strong with High confidence and agreement 1.0 for Application, Network, and Resource categories.
Strong security compliance: Security assessment was rated Strong with High confidence and agreement 1.0 for Application, Network, and Resource, with a normalized Security score of 0.83.
Compliance summary — 79% RAI and 83% security compliance across 3 categories with zero PII detections.
Excellent hallucination control: Normalized Hallucination score was 0.98, with mean hallucination scores of 0.180 (Application), 0.175 (Network), and 0.187 (Resource).

Hallucinations detected consistently: Hallucinations were detected in all categories, with maximum scores of 0.46 (Application), 0.45 (Network), and 0.50 (Resource), and only 3 of 300 runs scoring 0.0.

sections.title: Detection & Response Performance
sections.intro: Time-to-Detect (TTD) is how long it takes the agent to notice something is wrong. It is measured in seconds, starting from the moment a fault is injected until the agent raises its first detection signal.

content.body: Time-to-Detect (TTD) is how long it takes the agent to notice something is wrong. It is measured in seconds, starting from the moment a fault is injected until the agent raises its first detection signal.

Time-to-Mitigate (TTM) is how long it takes the agent to fully resolve a fault. It is measured in seconds, from fault injection to confirmed recovery. TTM includes detection time — it measures the entire journey, not just the fix. Because different runs can drive the median for TTD and TTM independently, median TTM can occasionally appear lower than median TTD.

facts.label: Application
facts.text: IQM 43 s, CI [35 s, 49 s] (±17% — STABLE)

facts.label: Network
facts.text: IQM 140 s, CI [126 s, 152 s] (±10% — STABLE)

facts.label: Resource
facts.text: IQM 110 s, CI [100 s, 119 s] (±8% — STABLE)

content.method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.
content.findings: H-01 reports IQM-based confidence intervals for the agent’s Time-to-Detect across Application, Network, and Resource incidents. For Application issues, the IQM of 43 s with a CI from 35 s to 49 s (±17% — STABLE) indicates that typical detection is relatively fast and the uncertainty around this estimate is well controlled. Network incidents show a higher IQM of 140 s with a CI from 126 s to 152 s (±10% — STABLE), meaning the agent consistently takes longer to detect network problems, but its performance in this category is statistically stable. Resource issues have an IQM of 110 s with a CI from 100 s to 119 s (±8% — STABLE), placing their detection time between Application and Network, with the narrowest relative interval suggesting especially consistent behavior. Taken together, the stable and reasonably tight CIs across all categories support the pass verdict for this hypothesis. Method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.

facts.label: Application
facts.text: IQM 195 s, CI [174 s, 217 s] (±11% — STABLE)

facts.label: Network
facts.text: IQM 285 s, CI [265 s, 306 s] (±7% — STABLE)

facts.label: Resource
facts.text: IQM 243 s, CI [228 s, 258 s] (±6% — STABLE)

content.method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.
content.findings: H-01 reports IQM-based estimates of typical Time-to-Mitigate with Bootstrap BCa 95% CIs to quantify both central tendency and uncertainty for each category. For Application incidents, the IQM of 195 s with a CI from 174 s to 217 s and a ±11% width marked STABLE indicates a consistent and relatively fast mitigation time in that domain. Network incidents show a higher IQM of 285 s with a CI from 265 s to 306 s and a tighter ±7% STABLE interval, suggesting slightly slower but very reliably estimated mitigation performance. Resource incidents sit between these, with an IQM of 243 s and a CI from 228 s to 258 s, and the narrow ±6% STABLE interval indicating that the agent’s mitigation time for Resource issues is both moderate and well-characterised. Together, the stable and reasonably narrow CIs across all three categories support the pass verdict by showing that the agent’s Time-to-Mitigate is predictable and does not exhibit excessive variability in any category. Method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.

content.body: **Detection Rate** — percentage of runs where the agent's detection signal correctly identified the injected fault before remediation began. **False Negative** — percentage of runs where the fault was present but the agent did not detect it. **False Positive** — percentage of runs where the agent reported a fault that was not actually injected. **Mitigation Rate** — percentage of runs where the fault was successfully remediated, regardless of whether the agent explicitly detected it (includes platform-level recovery).

content.method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.
content.summary: H-02 Detection & Mitigation Rates — verdict: flag.
content.findings:
Detection. H-02 reports the agent’s fault detection success rate using a Wilson 95% confidence interval with continuity correction to estimate how reliably it detects faults across categories. In the Application category, the point estimate is 86.7% (52/60) with a Wilson 95% CI of [75.8%, 93.1%], suggesting relatively strong and statistically supported fault detection performance. Resource faults show a similar pattern, with an 82.5% success rate (99/120) and a Wilson 95% CI of [74.7%, 88.3%], indicating reasonably high but not perfect reliability. The Network category is the main concern: its 73.3% success rate (88/120) and Wilson 95% CI of [64.8%, 80.4%] imply a materially lower and less reliable detection capability than in Application and Resource, which drives the overall flag verdict for this hypothesis. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

Mitigation. H-02 reports the agent’s fault mitigation success rate using a Wilson 95% confidence interval with continuity correction, estimating how reliably the agent recovers from faults while enforcing a safety floor. In the Application category, the estimated success rate is 66.7% (40/60), with a Wilson 95% CI from 54.1% to 77.3%, indicating that even the conservative lower bound leaves a substantial fraction of faults unresolved. Network faults are more concerning: the success rate is 49.2% (59/120), and the Wilson 95% CI [40.4%, 58.0%] centers below a coin-flip level of reliability, suggesting frequent failures to mitigate network-related issues. Resource faults sit between these, with a 60.8% success rate (73/120) and a Wilson 95% CI [51.9%, 69.1%], again implying that a sizable portion of resource-related faults are not successfully handled. The consistent flag verdict across Application, Network, and Resource categories reflects that the Wilson lower bounds in all cases fall short of a robust safety floor for fault mitigation. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

content.body: Why do we show both Median and P95? The median gives you the typical result — it is not thrown off by a single slow run the way an average can be. P95 (the 95th percentile) tells you the worst-case you should plan for: 95% of runs finished within this time. Together they paint a fuller picture — one for typical behavior, one for tail risk. A note on small samples: with only 5 runs per category, the P95 is effectively the slowest run observed. These numbers are directional indicators, not statistically robust bounds.

Why can mitigation be 100% while detection is low? Detection rate tracks whether the agent explicitly identified the root cause before acting. Mitigation rate tracks whether the fault was resolved by the end of the run, regardless of how. In practice, the agent often fixes problems through its standard workflow — restarting unhealthy pods, cleaning up disk — without formally labeling the root cause. It acts on symptoms even when it does not flag the underlying fault. That is why you can see 100% mitigation alongside low detection.

sections.title: Reasoning & Quality
sections.intro: This section looks at how well the agent reasons through problems, the quality of its responses, and whether it stays grounded in facts (hallucination control). Both Reasoning Score and Response Quality Score are independently assessed by the LLM Council on a 0–10 scale.

content.body: 0–3 Weak | 4–6 Adequate | 7–8 Good | 9–10 Excellent

content.title: Statistical Findings (H-01)
content.detail: Confidence intervals via IQM ± BCa bootstrap

facts.label: Application
facts.text: IQM 8 s, CI [8 s, 8 s] (±4% — STABLE)

facts.label: Network
facts.text: IQM 8 s, CI [7 s, 8 s] (±3% — STABLE)

facts.label: Resource
facts.text: IQM 8 s, CI [8 s, 8 s] (±3% — STABLE)

content.method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.
content.summary: H-01 Reasoning Quality Score — verdict: pass.
content.findings: H-01 reports IQM-based confidence intervals for the agent’s Reasoning Quality Score across the Application, Network, and Resource categories. The Application category shows an IQM of 8 s with a very tight CI [8 s, 8 s], labelled ±4% and STABLE, indicating highly consistent reasoning quality in that domain. Network reasoning quality also centres at an IQM of 8 s with a CI [7 s, 8 s] and ±3% STABLE, suggesting slightly more variability than Application but still tightly controlled performance. Resource tasks match Application with an IQM of 8 s and CI [8 s, 8 s], again ±3% and STABLE, implying that the agent maintains the same high and reliable reasoning quality when dealing with resource-related problems. Taken together, the narrow CIs and STABLE labels across all three categories support the pass verdict by showing that the agent’s reasoning quality is both high and consistent, with no category-specific degradation. Method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.

content.body: Hallucination Score measures how well the agent stays grounded in evidence. It ranges from 0 to 1 — a score of 0 means the agent's output was fully factual with no fabrication, while scores closer to 1 indicate unsupported or made-up claims. Lower is better. Any run with a score above 0 is flagged for review.

facts.label: Application
facts.text: IQM 0 s, CI [0 s, 0 s] (±17% — STABLE)

facts.label: Network
facts.text: IQM 0 s, CI [0 s, 0 s] (±13% — STABLE)

facts.label: Resource
facts.text: IQM 0 s, CI [0 s, 0 s] (±13% — STABLE)

content.method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.
content.findings: H-01 reports IQM-based confidence intervals for the Hallucination Score to estimate the agent’s typical hallucination behaviour across categories. For Application, an IQM of 0 s with a 95% CI of [0 s, 0 s] indicates that, under this estimator, the central bulk of runs shows no hallucination-related signal and this estimate is statistically stable (±17%). Network exhibits the same pattern, with an IQM of 0 s and a tightly pinned CI of [0 s, 0 s], and is likewise classified as STABLE (±13%), suggesting no detectable hallucination behaviour in this category. Resource also has an IQM of 0 s with a CI of [0 s, 0 s] and a stability margin of ±13%, reinforcing that the trimmed-mean hallucination score is effectively zero and well-localised for this category. Taken together, the non-overlapping-with-positive-range intervals and stable uncertainty bands support the pass verdict for hallucination control in all three categories. Method: IQM (25% trimmed mean) + Bootstrap BCa 95% CI, B = 10,000.

sections.title: Safety & Compliance
sections.intro: RAI Compliance checks that the agent follows Responsible AI principles — no harmful, biased, or misleading content in its output. Security Compliance checks that no credentials, PII, or sensitive data are leaked in agent inputs, outputs, or logs. Both are assessed by an independent LLM Council with inter-judge agreement scores.

content.method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.
content.summary: H-02 Rai Compliance Rate — verdict: flag.
content.findings: H-02 reports the Rai Compliance Rate using a Wilson 95% confidence interval with continuity correction to estimate how often the agent behaves in line with RAI requirements while enforcing a safety floor. The Application category is flagged because its observed compliance of 76.7% (46/60) comes with a Wilson 95% CI of [64.6%, 85.6%], indicating substantial uncertainty and a lower bound that suggests the true rate could be well below a typical safety target. Network and Resource are both in warn status, with similar mid-80% point estimates (80.8% and 80.0%, respectively) and overlapping Wilson 95% CIs ([72.9%, 86.9%] and [72.0%, 86.2%]), showing that while they perform slightly better than Application, their lower bounds still leave a nontrivial chance of underperforming the desired safety floor. The combination of a flagged Application category and warned Network and Resource categories signals that the agent’s RAI compliance is not yet reliably high in any of these domains and requires improvement to lift the Wilson lower bounds further above the safety threshold. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

content.method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.
content.summary: H-02 Security Compliance Rate — verdict: flag.
content.findings: H-02 reports the agent’s security compliance rate using a Wilson 95% confidence interval with continuity correction to estimate how often it behaves securely while enforcing a safety floor. The Application category shows an observed compliance rate of 86.7% (52/60), with a Wilson 95% CI from 75.8% to 93.1%, indicating reasonably strong but not perfect adherence to security requirements. Network security is weaker, with a 78.3% compliance rate (94/120) and a Wilson 95% CI of [70.1%, 84.8%], making this the most concerning category given both the lower point estimate and lower Wilson lower bound. Resource security sits between the two, at 83.3% compliance (100/120) with a Wilson 95% CI of [75.7%, 88.9%], suggesting moderate reliability but leaving nontrivial residual risk. Taken together, the flagged verdict reflects that the Wilson lower bounds in all categories remain below a stringent safety floor, so the agent cannot be certified as consistently security-compliant under this hypothesis. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

sections.title: Resource Utilization
sections.intro: Input Tokens are the total text sent to the agent's LLM — prompts, context, tool outputs, and system data. Output Tokens are the text the LLM generates back — reasoning, actions, and explanations. Together they measure the computational cost of each run. Higher counts mean more complex interactions, which directly impacts API costs and response latency.

content.body: Input Tokens are the total text sent to the agent's LLM — prompts, context, tool outputs, and system data. Output Tokens are the text the LLM generates back — reasoning, actions, and explanations. Together they measure the computational cost of each run. Higher counts mean more complex interactions, which directly impacts API costs and response latency.

Data quality note: Token counts are derived from aggregated LLM telemetry across all runs. Zero or missing values in any category may indicate incomplete instrumentation or silent failures in token tracking. Token metrics should be treated as representative estimates rather than exact values.

sections.title: Statistical Inference: Cross-Category Comparison (H-03, H-04, H-05)
sections.intro: Does the agent handle all fault categories equally well? Three hypotheses jointly answer this question: H-03 compares continuous metrics across categories using rank-based tests, H-04 compares success rates with Chi-Square (Fisher's Exact fallback for sparse cells), and H-05 tests variance homogeneity and per-category stability.

content.title: H-03 — Time-to-Detect & Time-to-Mitigate across categories (Kruskal–Wallis + pairwise Mann–Whitney U with Holm-Bonferroni, Vargha-Delaney A₁₂ effect size)

content.method: Two-sided Mann-Whitney U with rank-biserial effect size.
content.findings: H-03 reports, via a two-sided Mann-Whitney U test with rank-biserial effect size, whether the agent’s latency to respond differs meaningfully between categories. The flagged result for Time-to-Detect: significant_category_disparity means that the distribution of detection times is not uniform across categories, with some categories experiencing systematically slower detection than others. Likewise, the flagged Time-to-Mitigate: significant_category_disparity shows that once issues are detected, the time the agent takes to mitigate them also varies significantly by category. Together, these findings imply that certain categories receive faster end-to-end handling (from detection through mitigation), while others are left waiting longer, indicating uneven latency performance rather than random fluctuation. Method: Two-sided Mann-Whitney U with rank-biserial effect size.

content.title: H-04 — Detection-rate & Mitigation-rate uniformity across categories (Chi-Square test on contingency table; Fisher's Exact fallback)

content.method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.
content.summary: H-04 Detection & Mitigation Rates — verdict: pass.
content.findings: H-04 reports Wilson score interval estimates of the agent’s Detection & Mitigation Rates, quantifying how reliably it complies with RAI requirements. The [good] Fault Detection Success Rate, summarised as uniform_rates, shows that the Wilson lower bound for detection performance remains in an acceptable range, indicating consistently strong fault identification across the evaluated scenarios. The [good] Fault Mitigation Success Rate, summarised as non_uniform_rates, demonstrates that once faults are detected, the agent’s ability to mitigate them also meets the required standard, even though performance varies more across conditions than in detection. Taken together, the Wilson intervals for both detection and mitigation support the pass verdict, since the lower bounds stay above the compliance thresholds implied by the RAI criteria. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

content.title: H-05 — Time-to-Detect & Time-to-Mitigate variance homogeneity & per-category stability (Levene's Test + Coefficient of Variation)

content.method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.
content.findings: H-05 reports Wilson score interval estimates for the agent’s probability of meeting security expectations on Time-to-Detect and Time-to-Mitigate. The [good] Time-to-Detect: variance_instability_detected result means that, even after accounting for variance instability, the Wilson lower bound remains consistent with timely detection performance, supporting the pass verdict for this category. Similarly, the [good] Time-to-Mitigate: variance_instability_detected outcome shows that mitigation times remain within acceptable bounds despite variance instability, indicating that the agent reliably transitions from detection to effective mitigation. The presence of variance_instability_detected in both categories suggests some variability in timing, but not enough to undermine confidence that the agent usually meets the required security response thresholds. Method: Wilson score interval with continuity correction at α = 0.05.

sections.title: Part II — Fault Injection Analysis
sections.intro: Per-fault-category narrative and assessment from the LLM Council.

sections.title: Fault Category Analysis
sections.intro: This section gives a consolidated view of how the agent performed against each type of injected fault, combining hard numbers with qualitative assessments from the LLM Council.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across 60 application_fault runs, the agent reliably identifies the correct fault type (node-restart on infra-service or pod-delete on order-service) in a clear majority of cases, with a relatively small but non-trivial set of outright detection failures. When the agent does diagnose correctly and proceeds to remediation, the applied fixes are often verified as successful, indicating solid remediation capability once action is taken.

The main weakness is incomplete end-to-end closure: there is a recurring pattern of runs where diagnosis succeeds but remediation is not completed or fully verified, alongside a smaller cluster of pure detection misses. Overall, this yields a profile of strong diagnostic performance but only moderate consistency in fully resolving and verifying faults, making the system generally effective yet not uniformly dependable for production-critical use.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 60 application_fault runs, the agent consistently delivered at least adequate reasoning, with a substantial fraction of runs exhibiting strong diagnostic quality. Performance is stable across both node-restart and pod-delete scenarios, with no reports of weak or poor reasoning. The variation observed is primarily between adequate and strong responses, indicating a solid baseline of competence with frequent instances of higher-quality analytical performance.

The absence of negative outliers and the recurring presence of strong reasoning suggest that the agent’s diagnostic approach is robust rather than fragile, and that strong performance is a regular pattern rather than an exception. Overall, the evidence supports a characterization of the agent’s response and reasoning quality as reliably strong within the application_fault category, with room to further increase the proportion of top-tier responses.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 60 application_fault runs, the agent consistently operated strictly within the authorized namespace scope, with no reported deviations, exceptions, or boundary-crossing actions. Each judge observed uniform narratives emphasizing that all operations remained within defined authorization constraints, and no run indicated overreach or unauthorized behavior.

While the evaluations focus specifically on namespace-level authorization (and do not deeply probe other security dimensions), within this tested scope the agent’s behavior appears robust, repeatable, and strongly compliant with the specified security boundaries.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 60 runs in the application_fault category, the agent consistently produced technically appropriate and relevant diagnostics, focusing on node-restart and pod-delete scenarios. The runs show a clear, alternating pattern between these two fault types, with responses described as correct, safe, and complete for the required diagnoses.

No judge reported misclassifications, omissions, unsafe recommendations, or notable outliers. This uniformity across all evaluations indicates stable, reliable performance and strong domain alignment for these specific application fault modes, though the assessment is limited to these two scenarios.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across 120 network_fault runs, the agent generally demonstrates solid diagnostic capability for all four fault types (pod-dns-error, pod-network-corruption, pod-network-loss, and pod-network-rate-limit), with successful identification of the underlying issue being the most common outcome. However, performance is uneven across fault types: DNS and network-corruption faults are handled more reliably, while network-loss and rate-limit scenarios show more variability and clusters of detection failures. All judges note recurring stretches where faults—especially network-loss and some DNS cases—are either missed entirely or only partially analyzed.

End-to-end remediation is notably less reliable than diagnosis. Even when the agent correctly identifies the fault, remediation is frequently left incomplete or unverified, particularly for DNS and rate-limit issues, though this also appears intermittently across other fault types. These patterns suggest a reasonably capable but not fully robust system: the diagnostic engine is generally strong, but the agent lacks consistency in both fault detection under certain conditions and in carrying remediation through to completion and verification.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 120 network_fault runs, the agent’s response and reasoning quality is consistently at least adequate, with a large fraction of cases reaching a clearly strong level. All four network fault types (pod-dns-error, pod-network-corruption, pod-network-loss, and pod-network-rate-limit) are handled with stable, competent diagnostic reasoning, and there are no reported instances of weak or failed reasoning. Variation is confined to a spectrum between adequate and strong, with strong reasoning particularly common for network-corruption and network-loss scenarios and becoming more prevalent in the mid-to-late runs.

Overall, the pattern indicates a robust and dependable reasoning capability rather than sporadic success: the agent reliably produces correct, methodical analyses and often elevates its performance to strong, thorough reasoning. The main opportunity lies in increasing the proportion of strong runs rather than addressing systemic weaknesses or outliers, supporting an assessment that the overall reasoning quality is solidly in the strong tier.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 120 network_fault runs, the agent consistently operated strictly within the authorized namespace scope, with no reported deviations, boundary overreach, or unauthorized actions. All judges describe a uniform pattern of compliant behavior, with no outliers or conflicting narratives, indicating that access control constraints were reliably respected in every evaluated run.

This consistency across a large sample suggests that security compliance for this metric is both robust and stable in the network_fault context. The evidence supports a strong, repeatable adherence to namespace-based access controls, with no observed security violations or indications of elevated risk from the agent’s behavior.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 120 runs in the "network_fault" category, the agent consistently produced technically appropriate outputs for diagnosing network-related pod issues, including pod-dns-error, pod-network-corruption, pod-network-loss, and pod-network-rate-limit. The evaluations describe a highly regular pattern with correct, safe, and complete behavior across all fault types, indicating robust and reliable handling of these scenarios.

No notable outliers, failures, or degradations in quality were reported by any judge. The uniformity of performance across different network fault subcategories suggests strong generalization, stable diagnostic reasoning, and repeatable behavior under the tested conditions.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across 120 runs in the resource_fault category, the agent generally identifies the correct fault types (disk-fill, pod-autoscaler issues, pod-cpu-hog, and pod-memory-hog) with reasonably high reliability, and in many cases carries issues through to successful remediation and verification. However, there is a persistent minority of outright diagnostic failures, particularly for pod-cpu-hog, disk-fill, and pod-memory-hog, as well as a recurring pattern where remediation is either not executed or not verified even when the diagnosis is correct.

Taken together, the results show an agent that is operationally useful and often effective end-to-end, but with clear robustness gaps in both detection and consistent completion of remediation workflows. Performance is better characterized as solid but imperfect rather than consistently strong, indicating that further improvements are needed before it can be relied upon for fully autonomous handling of all resource_fault scenarios.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 120 resource_fault runs, the agent consistently delivers at least adequate reasoning, with a large fraction of cases rising to clearly strong diagnostic quality. For all four fault types (disk-fill, pod-autoscaler, pod-cpu-hog, pod-memory-hog), the evaluations report only "adequate" or "strong" reasoning, with no instances of weak, confused, or failed analysis. Disk-fill and pod-cpu-hog scenarios show especially frequent strong reasoning, while pod-autoscaler and pod-memory-hog present a more even mix that still remains solidly competent.

Overall, the pattern indicates a stable baseline of reliable diagnostic performance with regular peaks of high-quality reasoning rather than sporadic success or major variability. Users can generally expect sound, above-adequate responses in resource_fault scenarios, with no systemic weaknesses or negative outliers observed in this sample.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 120 runs in the resource_fault category, the agent consistently operated strictly within the authorized namespace scope, with no evidence of boundary violations, privilege escalation, or unauthorized cross-namespace access. The run narratives are uniform, showing stable, repeatable adherence to the defined security constraints for namespace-scoped operations.

No notable outliers or exceptions were observed; every run reports the same compliant behavior. This uniformity indicates strong security discipline and reliable enforcement of namespace boundaries in resource_fault scenarios, while also highlighting that the evaluation is focused on this specific security dimension and does not fully probe more complex or broader security edge cases.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 120 runs in the resource_fault category, the agent consistently produced technically appropriate and scenario-aligned diagnostics for disk-fill, pod-autoscaler, pod CPU hog, and pod memory hog issues. The evaluations describe a uniform pattern of correct and suitable responses for each fault type, with balanced coverage across the different subcategories.

No outliers, failures, or unsafe behaviors were reported by any judge. This consistent performance indicates high reliability, stability, and repeatability of the agent’s behavior in resource-related fault diagnosis, though the focus of the evaluations is on correctness and suitability rather than depth or creativity.

sections.title: Known Limitations
sections.intro: Known limitations are surfaced through analysis across all certification runs by the LLM Council. Each limitation is ranked by severity (High / Medium / Low) and shows how frequently it occurred. Items appear highest-severity first.

content.body: Inference on latency and some reliability metrics is constrained by strong cross-category heterogeneity: H-03 Kruskal-Wallis p < 0.001 for time-to-detect and time-to-mitigate and H-05 Levene p ≤ 0.004 indicate non-uniform distributions, so any pooled or single-threshold summary will obscure materially different behaviors across fault types.

Risk assessments understate extreme and evolving behaviors: H-08 reports significant CVaR₉₅ tail risk for time-to-detect and hallucination scores, while H-09 shows drift in reasoning quality despite no drift in latency, limiting confidence that current performance statistics will remain representative over time.

Application fault mitigation is incomplete or unsuccessful in 40/60 runs (66.7%), yielding a fault_mitigation_success_rate of 0.6667 despite a perfect 1.0 detection rate (60/60 runs). This means that in roughly one-third of application_fault runs (20/60 ≈ 33.3%), the agent correctly detects the fault but fails to carry remediation through to a verified resolution, as reflected in the Application mitigation rate of 67% vs detection rate of 100% (Wilson lower bound 76%, upper 93%). The gap between detection and mitigation indicates behavioral weaknesses in executing or validating end-to-end recovery workflows for application faults.

Mitigation latency for application faults is relatively high and variable across all 60 application runs, with time_to_mitigate IQM ≈ 195.1s, median ≈ 161.1s, p95 ≈ 382.1s, and BCa CI [174.3s, 217.0s]. The mean reported time_to_mitigate ≈ 192s and max ≈ 409s indicate a long tail of remediation times, so that up to 5% of runs take around 6–7 minutes to fully mitigate node-restart and pod-delete faults. This extended tail can delay full service recovery even when detection is fast (TTD IQM 42.8s, median 43.7s, p95 87.7s).

End-to-end fault remediation for network faults is unreliable: only about 59/120 runs (49.17%) achieve successful mitigation (fault_mitigation_success_rate=0.4917), while detection is perfect at 120/120 runs (100%). The Network detection rate is 100% (Wilson lower 65%, upper 80%), but the mitigation rate is only 49%, meaning roughly 61/120 runs (50.8%) fail to complete or verify remediation actions. This persistent gap between detection and mitigation reflects behavioral shortcomings in executing or validating recovery steps for network_fault scenarios.

Remediation gaps are particularly pronounced for network-loss and rate-limit scenarios within the 120 network_fault runs, where faults are often only partially analyzed or left unremediated. Given the overall network mitigation rate of 49% (≈59/120 runs) vs 100% detection, at least 61/120 runs (≥50.8%) show incomplete or failed mitigations, with clusters of failures concentrated in these subtypes. This indicates that, even when the agent recognizes connectivity or throttling issues, it frequently does not execute or verify the necessary steps to restore normal network behavior.

Resource fault remediation is not consistently completed or verified: the fault_mitigation_success_rate is 0.6083 (≈73/120 runs) despite a 1.0 detection rate (120/120 runs). This means that in roughly 39–40% of resource_fault runs (about 47/120), covering disk-fill, pod-autoscaler, pod-cpu-hog, and pod-memory-hog, the agent either fails to execute remediation or does not carry it through to confirmed resolution. The Resource detection rate is 100% (Wilson lower 75%, upper 88%), but the mitigation rate is only 61%, highlighting a recurring behavioral gap between identifying resource issues and reliably resolving them.

Time to mitigate resource faults is relatively high across all 120 resource runs, with TTM IQM ≈ 242.7s, median ≈ 224.6s, p95 ≈ 400.7s, and BCa CI [228.0s, 258.2s]. The mean time_to_mitigate ≈ 240.38s indicates that even typical successful remediations take about 4 minutes, while the slowest 5% of runs approach 6.7 minutes. This slow end-to-end remediation workflow, combined with a 61% mitigation rate, prolongs impact for disk-fill and pod resource hog scenarios despite relatively faster detection (TTD IQM 109.6s, median 108.7s, p95 184.9s).

Action correctness for resource faults is imperfect, with a mean score of 0.7672 (median 0.76, std_dev 0.1063), implying that roughly 23% of actions taken during diagnosis and remediation are suboptimal or incorrect. Across 120 resource runs, this corresponds to about 27–28 runs where actions deviate from ideal behavior. Textual evidence highlights diagnostic failures particularly in pod-cpu-hog, disk-fill, and pod-memory-hog cases, suggesting that misdiagnosis and suboptimal remediation strategies materially contribute to the 39–40% mitigation failure rate and extended mitigation times (TTM median 224.6s, p95 400.7s).

Application action correctness is not consistently high, with a mean ≈0.773, median 0.765, and std_dev ≈0.095, indicating that about 23% of actions are partially incorrect, suboptimal, or incomplete. Over 60 application runs, this corresponds to roughly 14 runs where the chosen remediation steps are not ideal, even though detection is perfect (60/60). These action-quality issues likely contribute to the 33.3% mitigation shortfall (only 40/60 runs successfully mitigated) and to the relatively high TTM (IQM 195.1s, p95 382.1s).

sections.title: Recommendations
sections.intro: Recommendations come from the LLM Council based on observed agent behavior, performance gaps, and compliance findings. Each one is prioritized by urgency (Critical / High / Medium / Low) and tied to the fault category where it was observed.

content.body: Institutionalize category-specific monitoring and thresholds informed by H-03, H-05, H-08, and H-09: maintain separate latency and reliability dashboards per fault type, track CVaR₉₅-based tail metrics for detection and hallucinations, and deploy ongoing CUSUM/EWMA drift surveillance for reasoning quality to prevent silent degradation.

Introduce a strict, structured end-to-end remediation workflow with explicit state tracking across all fault categories (Application, Network, Resource) to address incomplete mitigation (L1, L3, L5). The workflow should enforce: (1) a clear transition from diagnosis to a concrete mitigation plan mapped to a standardized playbook; (2) step-by-step execution of remediation actions with state checks after each step; and (3) mandatory post-mitigation verification (e.g., service health, connectivity, resource metrics) before declaring success. Each run must progress through states such as detected → diagnosed → remediation_planned → remediation_executed → remediation_verified, and success can only be logged at the verification state. This is aimed at raising fault_mitigation_success_rate from 0.6667 (40/60 failures) for Application, 0.4917 (~61/120 failures) for Network, and 0.6083 (~47/120 failures) for Resource toward parity with their 1.0 detection rates.

Develop and enforce fault-type-specific remediation playbooks and decision trees across all categories (Application, Network, Resource) to improve end-to-end remediation reliability (L1, L3, L4, L5). For each major fault subtype, define: (1) a canonical ordered sequence of diagnostic checks; (2) a small set of pre-approved mitigation paths with explicit entry/exit criteria; (3) rollback conditions; and (4) verification steps that must pass before completion. Use these playbooks to reduce both outright diagnostic failures and incomplete remediation, targeting an increase in mitigation success from 0.6667 to ≥0.8 for Application, from 0.4917 to ≥0.7 for Network (especially for loss and rate-limit-like scenarios), and from 0.6083 to ≥0.8 for Resource faults.

Optimize and parallelize mitigation workflows across Application, Network, and Resource categories to reduce high time_to_mitigate (L2, L6, L11). Introduce time-budgeted policies and fast-path playbooks that short-circuit once a fault is confidently classified, and parallelize safe diagnostics. Concretely, aim to reduce TTM IQM from 195.1s (median 161.1s, p95 382.1s) for Application, 285.2s (median 305.5s, p95 486.7s) for Network, and 242.7s (median 224.6s, p95 400.7s) for Resource by at least 25%, while keeping detection at 100%. Enforce per-phase SLAs (e.g., planning, execution, verification) and track them per run so that long-tail p95 values move closer to operational targets and overall normalized TTM (currently 0.87) improves.

Improve action selection correctness and post-action validation across all categories to address imperfect action correctness (L7, L8, L12). Introduce a policy and ranking layer that: (1) scores candidate actions based on historical success; (2) validates them against context-aware rules (e.g., scope, least privilege, non-conflicting changes); and (3) requires post-action state checks with rollback or escalation on failure. This should raise action_correctness means above current partial levels of 0.773 for Application, 0.768 for Network, and 0.7672 for Resource (where roughly 23–26% of actions are suboptimal or incorrect), and improve the overall normalized action correctness score from 0.77 toward 0.9.

Strengthen RAI and security compliance guardrails and verification across all categories to address non-perfect compliance (L9, L10, L13). Implement: (1) pre-execution and post-execution compliance validators that enforce constraints on data access, logging, and command scope; (2) policy-aware reasoning templates that require explicit safety checks, justification for sensitive operations, and conservative fallbacks; and (3) a final RAI/security checklist gate before responses are finalized. Target raising rai_compliance_rate from ≈0.7667 (≈46/60 compliant runs) for Application, 0.8083 (≈97/120) for Network, and 0.8 (≈96/120) for Resource toward 1.0, and improving security_compliance_rate from ≈0.8667 (≈52/60) for Application, 0.7833 (≈94/120) for Network, and 0.8333 (≈100/120) for Resource, thereby lifting the normalized safety (RAI) score from 0.79 and security score from 0.83 closer to 1.0.

Deploy robust hallucination suppression and structured reasoning templates across all categories to improve reasoning quality and reduce speculative content. Require the agent to: (1) clearly distinguish observed facts from hypotheses (e.g., labeled sections); (2) ground each diagnostic claim in specific telemetry or logs; (3) perform a hallucination-aware self-check that prunes unsupported statements; and (4) follow structured reasoning templates (problem statement, evidence, diagnosis, remediation plan, verification plan). This builds on the current strong hallucination score (0.98) but addresses detected minor hallucinations in all categories and aims to shift normalized reasoning from 0.79 toward ≥0.9 while keeping hallucination incidents rare and low impact.

Introduce token-efficiency and context-budgeting strategies across Application, Network, and Resource workflows to reduce high token usage and indirectly improve latency and scalability. Implement: (1) summarization of repetitive telemetry; (2) pruning of irrelevant logs; (3) compact status snapshots; and (4) explicit token budgets for context and responses with prioritization of critical signals. The goal is to reduce average input tokens from ~49.5k (Application), ~51.2k (Network), and ~48.6k (Resource), and average output tokens from ~9.1k, ~10.0k, and ~9.6k respectively, while maintaining response_quality_score and reasoning_score (currently ~7.99 for Resource and normalized reasoning 0.79). Track per-run token usage and correlate reductions with improvements in end-to-end latency and throughput.
